shortDescription: The primary dwarven expletive in Dragon Age. Already a real (mild) British curse, it fits naturally for the dwarven culture.
etymologyNarrative: Borrowed from real British English where 'sod' (from 'sodomite') is a mild expletive. BioWare gave it to the dwarves, making it feel authentic within a fantasy setting that uses British-inflected English.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the Dragon Age series. Primarily associated with dwarven characters.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Dragon Age: Origins (2009), Dragon Age II (2011), and Dragon Age: Inquisition (2014).
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Functions at its real-world British level of mild vulgarity. Effective because it's already a real word, giving it instant authenticity.
regionalNotes: Dwarven. Associated with Orzammar and the dwarven caste system.

exampleQuote.text: Sodding darkspawn! They're everywhere!

shortDescription: A dwarven insult implying someone engages in sexual acts with nugs (small, hairless rabbit-like creatures). The dwarven equivalent of an extreme sexual insult.
etymologyNarrative: Nugs are small, unappetizing creatures kept as pets or food in dwarven society. Accusing someone of intimate relations with them is maximally degrading.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent extreme insult throughout the Dragon Age series.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Dragon Age: Origins and subsequent games. Part of BioWare's commitment to creating culturally specific insults for each fantasy race.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: One of the strongest insults available in Dragon Age. Combining bestiality implications with the specific dwarven cultural context.
regionalNotes: Dwarven. Used primarily in Orzammar and the Deep Roads.

exampleQuote.text: Listen here, you sodding nug-humper!

shortDescription: A milder dwarven insult comparing someone's intelligence to that of a nug — a small, simple creature.
etymologyNarrative: Compound of 'nug' (the creatures) and 'head.' Like calling someone a 'birdbrain' or 'donkey.'
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent mild insult in dwarven vocabulary.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used across the Dragon Age series.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mild for dwarven standards. The milder end of nug-based insults.
regionalNotes: Dwarven.

exampleQuote.text: Don't be such a nughead, Oghren.

firstAppearance: Words of Radiance by Brandon Sanderson (2014)
shortDescription: Lift's personal curse word in the Stormlight Archive. Reflects her constant preoccupation with food and hunger.
etymologyNarrative: Lift is perpetually hungry (she converts food to Stormlight). Her personal curse word reflecting her greatest concern is a masterful character detail by Sanderson.
semanticDriftTimeline: Specific to Lift. Other characters don't use it as profanity. Remains her personal verbal tic throughout her appearances.
usageHistoryNarrative: Introduced with Lift's character. Used in Edgedancer and Rhythm of War.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Works specifically because of Lift's character. It would feel wrong from any other character, making it one of the most character-specific fictional curses.
regionalNotes: Specific to Lift. Other Rosharans use 'storming.'

exampleQuote.text: That was so starving awesome!

firstAppearance: The Way of Kings by Brandon Sanderson (2010)
shortDescription: An oath invoking Kelek, one of the ten Heralds (quasi-divine figures) of the Stormlight Archive. Using a Herald's name in vain is blasphemous.
etymologyNarrative: Kelek (also Kelek'Elin) is one of the ten Heralds of the Almighty. Using divine names as profanity follows the same pattern as 'Christ' or 'God's blood' in English.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent blasphemous exclamation. Other Herald names appear in similar oaths: 'Ash's eyes,' 'Damnation,' etc.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Stormlight Archive. Part of a system where each Herald's name can be invoked in different oath constructions.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Moderate blasphemy. In a world where the Heralds are genuine historical figures, invoking them casually is disrespectful but extremely common.
regionalNotes: Used across Vorin-practicing nations on Roshar.

exampleQuote.text: Kelek's breath! What happened here?

firstAppearance: Only Superhuman by Christopher L. Bennett (2012)
shortDescription: The primary expletive among Striders (asteroid belt dwellers). Short for 'vacuum' — the ever-present lethal threat of space.
etymologyNarrative: Short for 'vacuum,' which is an existential threat to space dwellers. In a society with fewer sexual taboos, sex-based profanity lost its power, and the thing people actually fear (explosive decompression) replaced it.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent within the novel. Represents a deliberate evolution in what cultures consider taboo.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Only Superhuman (2012). Notable for its thoughtful approach to how profanity evolves based on cultural fears.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: A fascinating example of how taboo words might shift based on what a culture actually fears. Sexual terms became mundane; mortal danger became profane.
regionalNotes: Asteroid Belt / Strider culture.

exampleQuote.text: What the vack just happened?

shortDescription: The primary expletive in the city of Tevanne. Functions as the world's F-word.
etymologyNarrative: Part of the Tevanne vocabulary in a world dominated by scriving (magical inscription). The word's sound evokes both 'scrum' and 'screw.'
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the Founders trilogy.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in the Founders trilogy (2018-2021). Helps establish Tevanne as a distinct culture.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Functions as a strong expletive in a setting that otherwise feels Mediterranean-influenced. Its unfamiliarity constantly reminds the reader they're in another world.
regionalNotes: Tevanne and surrounding city-states.

exampleQuote.text: Scrum this. We're getting out of here.

shortDescription: Created when Willow spelled out 'B-I-T-C-H' and Xander misheard/mispronounced it as 'bitca.' Became a running joke.
etymologyNarrative: A meta-linguistic joke. Willow tries to be discreet by spelling the word. Xander's mispronunciation turns it into a new word. Classic Buffy humor about language itself.
semanticDriftTimeline: A brief comedic moment that fans latched onto. Never became the characters' standard vocabulary.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used briefly in Buffy's early seasons. Beloved by fans as a quintessential Buffy-speak moment.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Not truly taboo — it's a comedic mispronunciation. Its charm is in the character dynamic, not the word's severity.
regionalNotes: Sunnydale, California. Scooby Gang in-joke.

exampleQuote.text: Xander: Bitca? Willow: I was spelling it out...

shortDescription: A Narn word roughly equivalent to 'shit' or 'crap.' Used by Narn characters on Babylon 5.
etymologyNarrative: From the Narn language. Part of Babylon 5's effort to give each alien race distinctive vocabulary.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent Narn expletive throughout the series.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Babylon 5's five-season run (1994-1998).
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: A moderate scatological term. Part of B5's more restrained approach to fictional profanity compared to contemporaries.
regionalNotes: Narn. Used by members of the Narn Regime.

exampleQuote.text: Shrok! They've found us!

shortDescription: One of SpongeBob's many nautical-themed profanity substitutions. Part of a system including 'barnacles,' 'tartar sauce,' and 'fish paste.'
etymologyNarrative: SpongeBob SquarePants systematically replaces all profanity with sea-themed equivalents. 'Holy shrimp' replaces 'holy shit' following the same pattern as 'oh for cod's sake' and 'what the halibut.'
semanticDriftTimeline: Part of an evolving system. New nautical substitutions are added each season while old ones persist.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used across SpongeBob's 14+ seasons (1999-present). The 'Sailor Mouth' episode (Season 2) made the show's profanity substitution system itself the subject of comedy.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Deliberately mild — it's a children's show. But the substitution system is so consistent that adult viewers understand exactly what's being replaced.
regionalNotes: Bikini Bottom and surrounding undersea territories.

exampleQuote.text: Holy shrimp! What happened to the Krusty Krab?

shortDescription: SpongeBob's nautical equivalent of 'damn' or 'crap.' Barnacles are nuisance organisms that cling to surfaces underwater.
etymologyNarrative: Barnacles are real marine organisms that attach to surfaces and are difficult to remove. They're a genuine nuisance in marine environments, making them a natural choice for undersea profanity.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent nautical substitute throughout the series.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used across SpongeBob's entire run. Part of the show's systematic nautical profanity substitution.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mild children's show profanity. Effective because barnacles genuinely are annoying in a marine context.
regionalNotes: Bikini Bottom.

exampleQuote.text: Oh, barnacles! I forgot the secret formula!

shortDescription: A slightly stronger SpongeBob oath, typically reserved for moments of greater frustration than 'barnacles' warrants.
etymologyNarrative: Tartar sauce is a condiment served with fish — in the SpongeBob universe, this has cannibalistic overtones, potentially making it a darker oath than it appears.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent moderate oath in the SpongeBob universe.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used across SpongeBob's run. Slightly less frequent than 'barnacles.'
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Occupies a middle tier in SpongeBob's profanity hierarchy — above 'barnacles' but below the unspeakable dolphin-noise-censored words.
regionalNotes: Bikini Bottom.

exampleQuote.text: Oh, tartar sauce! Not again!

shortDescription: In the 'Sailor Mouth' episode, actual swearing is censored by dolphin noises. The joke is that SpongeBob and Patrick are saying terrible things, but we only hear dolphin chirps.
etymologyNarrative: A meta-commentary on TV censorship itself. The episode acknowledges that swear words exist in the SpongeBob universe but replaces them with dolphin sounds. At episode's end, actual dolphin sounds are mistaken for profanity.
semanticDriftTimeline: The dolphin-noise system was primarily a 'Sailor Mouth' episode device, though it's been referenced in later episodes.
usageHistoryNarrative: Introduced in 'Sailor Mouth' (Season 2, Episode 18, 2001). The episode is consistently rated one of SpongeBob's best and most clever.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: A brilliant deconstruction of fictional profanity itself. By centering an entire episode on swearing without ever revealing the words, the show comments on the arbitrariness of what we consider taboo.
regionalNotes: Bikini Bottom. Mr. Krabs notes there are 13 bad words, and they've apparently learned number 11.

exampleQuote.text: SpongeBob: Hello, Patrick. Lovely [dolphin noise] day we're having, isn't it?

shortDescription: The full insult Leia hurls at Han Solo. 'Scruffy-looking' plus 'nerf herder' creates a compound insult about both appearance and social status.
etymologyNarrative: Combines personal appearance criticism with occupational condescension. Leia attacks Han on two fronts — he looks bad AND he's low-class.
semanticDriftTimeline: Remained a one-scene insult that became one of the most quoted lines in Star Wars history.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in The Empire Strikes Back (1980). Became one of the most beloved exchanges in cinema. Referenced, parodied, and quoted endlessly.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mild but devastating in context because of Carrie Fisher's delivery and the romantic tension. The insult is more revealing of Leia's feelings than of Han's character.
regionalNotes: Used aboard the Rebel base on Hoth.

exampleQuote.text: Why, you stuck-up, half-witted, scruffy-looking nerf herder! — Who's scruffy-looking?

englishEquivalent: Any curse word (meta-censorship becomes the word)
firstAppearance: Known Space by Larry Niven (1970s); Futurama (1999)
shortDescription: In Larry Niven's Known Space, the censor bleep itself evolved into a swear word. Also used in Futurama where characters literally say 'bleep.'
etymologyNarrative: A meta-linguistic evolution where the sound used to censor profanity became profanity itself. Niven's Known Space posits this as a natural linguistic evolution. Futurama uses it as comedy.
semanticDriftTimeline: In Known Space, it's presented as a natural linguistic development — the euphemism treadmill taken to its logical conclusion. In Futurama, it's played for laughs.
usageHistoryNarrative: Concept introduced in Niven's Known Space. Popularized comedically in Futurama and other shows.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: One of the most linguistically insightful fictional profanity concepts. It demonstrates the euphemism treadmill — how substitutes inevitably acquire the taboo of what they replace.
regionalNotes: Galaxy-wide in Known Space. Various uses in other media.

exampleQuote.text: Censor. Bleep. They didn't used to be swear words. They were euphemisms.

firstAppearance: The Eye of the World by Robert Jordan (1990)
shortDescription: A profane modifier in the Wheel of Time, used to intensify insults and express frustration. Often paired with 'bloody.'
etymologyNarrative: Possibly derived from the imagery of fire and destruction. In a world where channeling (magic) can cause literal flames, the word may carry additional weight.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent modifier throughout all 14 books. Often combined with 'bloody' for emphasis.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the entire Wheel of Time series (1990-2013) and the TV adaptation.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Moderate profanity. Stronger than 'bloody' alone but not the strongest oath available.
regionalNotes: Used across the Westlands.

exampleQuote.text: Flaming Aes Sedai! They'll be the death of us all!

shortDescription: An insult implying someone or something is as annoying as a person who kisses goats. Combines animal-related degradation with mild sexual implication.
etymologyNarrative: Combines pastoral animal imagery with mild sexual impropriety. In the Westlands' agrarian culture, accusing someone of inappropriate animal relations is a potent insult.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the series as a colorful modifier.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used scattered throughout the Wheel of Time series.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Moderate. Stronger than standard insults but still within the series' generally restrained profanity system.
regionalNotes: Common in the Two Rivers and other rural areas of the Westlands.

exampleQuote.text: That goat-kissing Son of the Sands!

shortDescription: Night City slang for killing someone, derived from the flatline on a heart monitor indicating death.
etymologyNarrative: From medical terminology — when a heart monitor shows a flat line, the patient is dead. In Night City's violent culture, it became a casual verb for murder.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent from the 1988 TTRPG through Cyberpunk 2077 and Edgerunners. Became more widely known through the 2020 game.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used since the original TTRPG. Central to Cyberpunk 2077's vocabulary. The in-game achievement system is literally called 'Flatlined.'
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Not technically a curse word but a violent slang term. Its casual use reflects Night City's extreme desensitization to violence.
regionalNotes: Universal across Night City.

exampleQuote.text: Cross me and I'll flatline you, choom.

shortDescription: Night City slang for murder. Reducing a life to zero — complete erasure. More deliberate and cold-blooded than 'flatline.'
etymologyNarrative: To reduce someone to zero — nothing. More clinical than 'flatline,' implying deliberate erasure rather than just death.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent from TTRPG through video game. 'Zeroed' is the past tense form used most commonly.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used since the original TTRPG. Central to the nihilistic vocabulary of Night City.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Extremely cold-blooded in implication. 'Flatline' at least references biology; 'zero' reduces a person to mathematics.
regionalNotes: Universal across Night City.

exampleQuote.text: People aren't zeroed if they die peacefully. It's for murders.

shortDescription: Various alien expletives used across the Star Trek franchise. Star Trek generally uses untranslated alien curses rather than invented English substitutes.
etymologyNarrative: Star Trek's approach differs from BSG or Farscape — rather than creating English-sounding substitute words, it uses untranslated alien phrases whose meaning is inferred from context.
semanticDriftTimeline: Varies by series and author. Star Trek's approach to profanity has evolved from the original series' complete avoidance to Discovery's use of actual English profanity.
usageHistoryNarrative: Scattered across decades of Star Trek media. Each series handles profanity differently.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Star Trek's trajectory mirrors real-world broadcast standards — from no profanity to alien substitutes to actual English swearing in streaming-era series.
regionalNotes: Species-specific throughout the Trek universe.

exampleQuote.text: Frinx! Quark gasped.

shortDescription: The emphatic form of 'gorram,' adding the '-it' suffix just as 'goddamn' becomes 'goddammit.'
etymologyNarrative: Natural grammatical extension of 'gorram.' Follows the same pattern as its real-world equivalent.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used alongside 'gorram' throughout Firefly and Serenity.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout Firefly (2002-2003) and Serenity (2005).
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Slightly stronger than 'gorram' alone due to the emphatic suffix.
regionalNotes: Outer planets / frontier culture in the Firefly universe.

exampleQuote.text: Gorramit, Mal, we're humped!

shortDescription: The High Republic era's distinctive expletive, adding to the Star Wars galaxy's expanding vocabulary of fictional profanity.
etymologyNarrative: Created for the High Republic publishing initiative. Distinct from existing Star Wars curses, suggesting that profanity evolves over the centuries between the High Republic and the films.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used within the High Republic era. Represents linguistic evolution within Star Wars.
usageHistoryNarrative: Introduced in Star Wars: The High Republic comic books and novels (2021 onward).
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Moderate expletive. Its existence in a different era from 'kriff' and 'karabast' suggests realistic linguistic change over centuries.
regionalNotes: High Republic era (approximately 200 years before the films).

exampleQuote.text: Crikk! The Nihil are attacking!

shortDescription: A pirate-specific swear word from the Star Wars Outer Rim. Used as an F-word equivalent in criminal circles.
etymologyNarrative: Introduced by Timothy Zahn as part of Outer Rim pirate vocabulary. Zahn, who also created 'kriff,' clearly enjoys inventing Star Wars profanity.
semanticDriftTimeline: Limited to pirate/criminal contexts. Never gained the widespread use of 'kriff.'
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Allegiance (2007) and related Zahn novels.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Strong expletive associated specifically with criminal elements.
regionalNotes: Outer Rim pirate territories.

exampleQuote.text: Don't crink this up, or the boss will have our heads.

shortDescription: An adjectival F-word euphemism used to describe bleak or disastrous situations.
etymologyNarrative: Likely modeled on real-world euphemisms like 'farkled' or 'farked.' Used to describe anything from broken equipment to hopeless situations.
semanticDriftTimeline: Limited usage compared to 'kriffed.' Never became the dominant past-tense form.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in various Star Wars novels.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Strong expletive. Functions exactly like 'fucked' in negative contexts.
regionalNotes: Used across the galaxy. No species-specific origin noted.

exampleQuote.text: The engine's farkled — we're not going anywhere.

shortDescription: In the South Park episode where 'shit' is overused until it loses meaning, Cartman suggests 'mee krob' as a replacement bad word. It's a Thai appetizer, making the joke about the arbitrariness of linguistic taboo.
etymologyNarrative: Mee krob is a real Thai dish (sweet crispy noodles). South Park uses it to make a point about how any word can become taboo if society decides it is. The choice of a random food item parallels Adams's use of 'Belgium.'
semanticDriftTimeline: A one-episode joke that fans remember fondly. Never became recurring South Park vocabulary.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in 'It Hits the Fan' (S5E1, 2001). The episode said 'shit' 162 times, exploring how overuse drains taboo power.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: A satirical commentary on the arbitrary nature of taboo language. If 'shit' loses its power through overuse, any word — even a noodle dish — can take its place.
regionalNotes: South Park, Colorado (fictional).

exampleQuote.text: We're going to have to start saying other bad words like 'cock' and 'mee krob.'

realWorldEuphemisms: None specific — it's a Thai dish used as absurdist profanity

franchise: Game of Thrones / A Song of Ice and Fire
notableSpeaker: Tyrion Lannister (Peter Dinklage), Robert Baratheon, many others
firstAppearance: A Game of Thrones by George R.R. Martin (1996) / TV S1E1 (2011)
shortDescription: The most iconic world-specific oath in Westeros. Invokes the Seven Hells of the Faith of the Seven, the dominant religion. Used to express shock, frustration, or anger.
etymologyNarrative: Derives from the theology of the Faith of the Seven, which teaches that there are seven heavens and seven hells. Invoking them is blasphemous in-universe but extremely common, much as 'hell' is used casually in English despite its religious origins.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent from the first book through the final TV season. Used by characters of all social classes, though highborn characters tend to use it less frequently than soldiers and commoners.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout all five ASOIAF novels (1996-2011) and eight seasons of HBO's Game of Thrones (2011-2019). One of the few world-specific oaths in a franchise that primarily uses real English profanity.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Martin deliberately mixes real English profanity with world-specific oaths. 'Seven Hells' is the standout invented term in a series that otherwise freely uses 'fuck,' 'shit,' and 'whore.' Its use signals Westerosi cultural identity.
regionalNotes: Used across Westeros wherever the Faith of the Seven is practiced. Followers of the Old Gods (Northerners) or the Drowned God (Ironborn) have their own oaths.

exampleQuote.text: Seven Hells, Cersei, what have you done?
exampleQuote.source: Game of Thrones / A Song of Ice and Fire

franchise: Game of Thrones / A Song of Ice and Fire
firstAppearance: A Game of Thrones by George R.R. Martin (1996)
shortDescription: A curse invoking the Others (White Walkers) — the ancient ice demons of Westerosi legend. Equivalent to 'the Devil take you' but using the world's own mythology.
etymologyNarrative: The Others are the great evil of Westerosi mythology — ice creatures from the Long Night. Invoking them is like cursing someone with the worst supernatural fate imaginable.
semanticDriftTimeline: In early books/seasons, used casually as the Others are considered legend. Gains terrifying weight as the White Walkers are confirmed real.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the ASOIAF series. Takes on dramatic irony as the story progresses and the Others become a genuine existential threat.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Fascinating trajectory — starts as a casual oath referencing mythology, then becomes genuinely terrifying as the myth proves real. Like casually saying 'the Devil take you' and then meeting the actual Devil.
regionalNotes: Used across the Seven Kingdoms. More common in the North where legends of the Others persist. Southerners use it but don't believe in the Others.

exampleQuote.text: The Others take your mild snows and bury you in them.
exampleQuote.source: Game of Thrones / A Song of Ice and Fire

franchise: Game of Thrones / A Song of Ice and Fire
firstAppearance: A Game of Thrones by George R.R. Martin (1996)
shortDescription: A Dothraki word meaning 'foreigner' used with deep contempt. The Dothraki consider all non-Dothraki peoples inferior, and 'ifak' carries that cultural supremacy.
etymologyNarrative: From the Dothraki language (developed by linguist David Peterson for the TV series from Martin's original fragments). Reflects the Dothraki's nomadic warrior culture that looks down on settled peoples.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent pejorative throughout the series. Daenerys's arc involves being initially dismissed as an ifak before earning Dothraki respect.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in the books and extensively in the HBO series. Part of the constructed Dothraki language that was one of the show's signature features.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Carries the full weight of cultural supremacy. In Dothraki society, being labeled an ifak means you're considered less than human — a 'walker' rather than a rider.
regionalNotes: Used exclusively by Dothraki speakers on the Great Grass Sea and wherever khalasars roam.

exampleQuote.text: She is no true khaleesi. She is an ifak who does not belong.
exampleQuote.source: Game of Thrones / A Song of Ice and Fire

notableSpeaker: Belter characters, especially Anderson Dawes (Jared Harris)
firstAppearance: Leviathan Wakes by James S.A. Corey (2011) / TV S1E1 (2015)
shortDescription: Belter Creole term for Earthers and Martians — the 'Inners' who exploit Belt resources while Belters live in poverty. One of the most politically charged slurs in modern sci-fi.
etymologyNarrative: From Belter Creole, a constructed language by linguist Nick Farmer. Combines 'inner' (the inner planets) with Belter grammatical suffixes. Carries centuries of colonial resentment.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the series. As political tensions escalate, the word gains increasingly revolutionary connotations.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Expanse novels (2011-2021) and the Amazon TV series (2015-2022). Central to the show's themes of colonialism, class warfare, and resource exploitation.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Deeply political. Not just a slur but a declaration of identity and resistance. Using 'inyalowda' signals Belter solidarity and rejection of Inner Planet authority.
regionalNotes: Used throughout the Belt — Ceres, Eros, Tycho Station, and outer planet settlements. Different Belt stations have slightly different pronunciations.

exampleQuote.text: Inyalowda have been exploiting beltalowda for generations.

notableSpeaker: Various Belters, directed at Detective Miller and others
firstAppearance: Leviathan Wakes by James S.A. Corey (2011) / TV S1E1 (2015)
shortDescription: A Belter who is overly sympathetic to or collaborates with the Inner Planets. The worst thing one Belter can call another — it means you've betrayed your own people.
etymologyNarrative: From Belter Creole. Possibly combines elements suggesting 'well-wishing' toward the enemy. Applied to any Belter who seems too comfortable with Inner authority.
semanticDriftTimeline: Detective Miller is called welwala early in the series for working with Earth-based authorities. The term gains weight as Belter independence movements intensify.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Expanse series. Detective Miller's arc involves being branded a welwala and having to reckon with where his loyalties truly lie.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: The most personally devastating insult for a Belter. It attacks not just behavior but identity — saying you're no longer truly one of your people.
regionalNotes: Used throughout the Belt. Especially cutting on stations with strong OPA (Outer Planets Alliance) presence.

exampleQuote.text: Miller? That welwala works for Star Helix. He's forgotten what it means to be beltalowda.

shortDescription: Belter Creole expletive derived from Chinese. Functions as the Belt's F-word.
etymologyNarrative: From Chinese 爬上 (páshàng), meaning 'to mount' or 'to climb on.' In Belter Creole, it evolved into a general-purpose sexual/obscene expletive. Reflects the multicultural origins of Belt colonists.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent expletive throughout the series. Used in compounds like 'pashangwala' (a more extreme variant).
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Expanse TV series. Part of the fully developed Belter Creole constructed language.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Functions identically to real-world strong profanity. The Chinese linguistic root gives it authenticity as a creole-language development.
regionalNotes: Used throughout the Belt. More common on Ceres and older Belt stations.

exampleQuote.text: Pashang! The air recyclers are failing!

firstAppearance: The Fellowship of the Ring by J.R.R. Tolkien (1954) / Film (2001)
shortDescription: Gandalf's signature rebuke. 'Fool' is practically a Tolkien curse word — the strongest language the wizard regularly uses. Directed at Peregrin Took after he drops a stone down a well in Moria.
etymologyNarrative: Tolkien deliberately avoided modern profanity, giving his characters archaic or elevated speech. 'Fool' is Gandalf's go-to insult, and combining it with 'Took' (Pippin's family name) makes it personal. The word 'fool' appears dozens of times in LOTR.
semanticDriftTimeline: 'Fool' is Gandalf's constant refrain — 'Fly, you fools!' being the most dramatic instance. The specific 'Fool of a Took' became iconic through the Peter Jackson films.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in The Fellowship of the Ring (1954) and immortalized by Ian McKellen's delivery in the 2001 film. Has become one of the most quoted lines in fantasy cinema.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: In Tolkien's elevated register, 'fool' carries the weight that harsher words carry in other fiction. Gandalf saying 'fool' hits as hard as another character dropping an F-bomb.
regionalNotes: Used by Gandalf throughout Middle-earth. The insult is sharpened by invoking Pippin's family name.

exampleQuote.text: Fool of a Took! Throw yourself in next time, and rid us of your stupidity!

shortDescription: Bilbo's exasperated outburst when dwarves invade his home. Pure Hobbit-style cursing — flustered, comical, and endearingly mild.
etymologyNarrative: Tolkien-invented compound of 'confusticate' (blend of confuse and complicate) and 'bebother' (intensified form of 'bother'). Perfectly captures Hobbit culture: even at their most upset, they sound like irritated English country gentlemen.
semanticDriftTimeline: One of the earliest lines in The Hobbit. Sets the tone for Hobbit speech patterns throughout all of Tolkien's work.
usageHistoryNarrative: From The Hobbit (1937). Referenced in Peter Jackson's film adaptation (2012). Beloved by fans as the quintessential Hobbit exclamation.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: The mildest possible fictional profanity. Its charm is in how it reveals Hobbit culture — even their strongest curse words sound polite. It's the fantasy equivalent of 'oh fiddlesticks.'
regionalNotes: The Shire, Hobbiton. Hobbit speech patterns in general favor elaborate, mild expressions over crude ones.

exampleQuote.text: Confusticate and bebother these dwarves!

firstAppearance: The Two Towers by J.R.R. Tolkien (1954)
shortDescription: Part of the untranslated Orcish (Black Speech) profanity in LOTR. Tolkien deliberately left Orcish curses untranslated because they were too foul for his elevated narrative style.
etymologyNarrative: From Tolkien's Black Speech. The full phrase 'Uglúk u bagronk sha pushdug Saruman-glob búbhosh skai' was left untranslated by Tolkien because he considered it too vulgar. Fan translations include words like 'dung-filth' and 'cesspool.'
semanticDriftTimeline: Orcish profanity represents the lowest register of speech in Middle-earth. Tolkien made it clear that bodily-function cursing was a mark of Orcish behavior.
usageHistoryNarrative: Appears in The Two Towers (1954). One of the few instances of genuinely vulgar language in Tolkien, made more striking by being in an alien tongue.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Maximum taboo within Middle-earth. Tolkien constructed a hierarchy where Elvish was the most beautiful language and Black Speech the most foul. Orcish profanity sits at the absolute bottom.
regionalNotes: Used by Orcs throughout Mordor and wherever Orcish cultures exist.

exampleQuote.text: Uglúk u bagronk sha pushdug Saruman-glob búbhosh skai!

shortDescription: An Ozian malapropism meaning 'disgusted beyond belief.' Part of the musical Wicked's invented Ozian dialect where standard English words are exaggerated into fanciful new forms.
etymologyNarrative: An 'Ozification' of 'disgusted,' adding extra syllables for dramatic effect. Part of a systematic pattern in Wicked where Ozian culture embellishes language. Listed in the official Ozian Glossararium in 'Wicked: The Grimmerie.'
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent with the musical's approach throughout its run. The 2024 film adaptation brought these terms to a much wider audience.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used since the musical's Broadway opening in 2003. Gained massive new exposure through the 2024 film starring Ariana Grande and Cynthia Erivo.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Not truly taboo but part of a delightful linguistic system that makes Oz feel like a real place with its own speech patterns. The Ozian dialect became beloved by fans.
regionalNotes: Used throughout Oz, but the more elaborate malapropisms are associated with Gilikinese (upper-class) society, particularly students at Shiz University.

exampleQuote.text: I am disgusticified by your behavior!

relatedWords: No direct equivalent — Ozian malapropisms are unique

shortDescription: An Ozian term meaning something has been made fashionable or impressive. Part of the Ozian vocabulary that adds '-ified' or '-ify' to existing English words.
etymologyNarrative: Part of the Ozian Glossararium. The '-ified' suffix is a signature Ozian linguistic pattern, turning adjectives into exaggerated new forms.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the musical.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in the Wicked musical since 2003. Part of the broader Ozian vocabulary system.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Not taboo at all — it's positive slang. Included here because Ozian vocabulary functions as a complete alternate dialect, including exclamations that replace standard expressions.
regionalNotes: Used across Oz, especially in the Emerald City and at Shiz University.

exampleQuote.text: The Emerald City is absolutely swankified!

firstAppearance: Guardians of the Galaxy comics (2008 DnA run)
shortDescription: A Kree-origin expletive equivalent to 'damn.' Star-Lord uses it frequently in the comics, though the MCU films use standard English profanity instead.
etymologyNarrative: From the Kree language in Marvel Comics. Peter Quill adopted it during his time in space, reflecting how he absorbed alien culture after leaving Earth as a child.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used consistently in the comics. The MCU films opted for English profanity instead, making d'ast less well-known to general audiences.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used primarily in the 2008-2010 Guardians of the Galaxy comic series by Dan Abnett and Andy Lanning.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Moderate comic-book expletive. Less well-known than Marvel's other invented profanity like 'shock' (2099) due to the films using English cursing.
regionalNotes: Used throughout the Marvel cosmic setting. Kree in origin but adopted by various spacefaring characters.

exampleQuote.text: D'ast! The Milano's engines are shot!

shortDescription: The Marvel cosmic F-word equivalent. Used by various aliens and spacefaring characters. Can be conjugated as 'flarking' or 'flarknard.'
etymologyNarrative: Part of Marvel's cosmic vocabulary. Allows characters in all-ages comics to express intense frustration without using real profanity.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used consistently in Marvel cosmic comics. The MCU chose not to adopt it.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in various Guardians of the Galaxy and cosmic Marvel comics.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Functions as the strongest expletive available in Marvel's cosmic comics, where real profanity is restricted by Comics Code legacy standards.
regionalNotes: Used throughout the Marvel cosmic setting.

exampleQuote.text: What the flark just happened?!

englishEquivalent: Kiss my ass / Go to hell
shortDescription: Bender's signature catchphrase. Not an invented word but a robot-specific adaptation of 'kiss my ass' that became one of the most iconic lines in animated TV history.
etymologyNarrative: A robot's version of 'kiss my ass.' The addition of 'shiny metal' makes it species-specific — Bender is literally made of metal. It's profanity adapted to robot identity.
semanticDriftTimeline: Bender's catchphrase throughout all seasons and revival. Various episodes play with it — 'Bite my glorious golden ass,' 'Bite my colossal metal ass,' etc.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout Futurama's original run (1999-2003), films (2007-2009), revival (2023-present). One of the most quoted catchphrases in animated television.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Straddles the line between real and fictional profanity — 'ass' is a real mild curse, but the full phrase is uniquely Bender's and serves as robot-culture profanity.
regionalNotes: Bender uses it everywhere, from New New York to the edges of the universe.

exampleQuote.text: Bite my shiny metal ass!

shortDescription: In the Land of Ooo, 'Glob' (or Grob Gob Glob Grod, a four-faced deity) serves as the God-figure invoked in exclamations. Lumpy Space Princess's 'Oh my Glob' became the show's most quoted phrase.
etymologyNarrative: Glob is an actual deity in Adventure Time's mythology — one of the four-faced rulers of Mars. Using the deity's name as a casual exclamation follows the same pattern as using 'God' or 'Christ' in English.
semanticDriftTimeline: LSP's 'Oh my Glob' became increasingly iconic throughout the show's ten seasons, eventually becoming Adventure Time's most recognizable catchphrase.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout Adventure Time's ten seasons (2010-2018) and its spinoffs. 'Oh my Glob' became hugely popular in internet culture and on merchandise.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mild blasphemy in the Adventure Time universe. The show's irreverent tone means it's played for comedy rather than genuine offense.
regionalNotes: Used throughout the Land of Ooo. Lumpy Space Princess uses it most frequently.

exampleQuote.text: Oh my Glob, you guys! Drama bomb!

notableSpeaker: Geralt of Rivia (Henry Cavill / Liam Hemsworth / Doug Cockle)
firstAppearance: The Last Wish by Andrzej Sapkowski (1993, English translation 2007)
shortDescription: While The Witcher uses plenty of real profanity, 'ploughing' serves as the Continent's distinctive strong adjective, replacing the F-word in many contexts.
etymologyNarrative: Derived from agricultural 'ploughing' — a farming metaphor for sexual activity that evolved into a general-purpose expletive. Fits the Continent's medieval agrarian setting perfectly.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used consistently in the books, Netflix series, and CD Projekt Red games. The games in particular made 'ploughing' a fan-favorite term.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Sapkowski's original Polish novels (translated), the Netflix series (2019-present), and especially the Witcher video game trilogy (2007-2015). The games popularized it for English-speaking audiences.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Functions as the Continent's signature expletive. While the series also uses real profanity freely, 'ploughing' gives the world its own linguistic flavor.
regionalNotes: Used across the Continent, from Temeria to Skellige.

exampleQuote.text: Ploughing hell, Geralt! What have you gotten us into?

shortDescription: Minions speak in 'Minionese,' a gibberish language mixing real words from multiple languages. 'Banana' is their most famous exclamation, but they also use 'Bee-do' (alarm), 'Pwede na' (Can we start), and various sounds that imply profanity through tone and context.
etymologyNarrative: Minionese was created by director Pierre Coffin, who voices all Minions. It blends words from English, Spanish, French, Italian, Japanese, Korean, and pure gibberish. Like the Smurfs, meaning is conveyed through context and tone.
semanticDriftTimeline: The language expanded with each Despicable Me film and the Minions spin-offs. New words are added as needed, though 'banana' remains the constant.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used across all Despicable Me films (2010, 2013, 2017, 2024) and Minions films (2015, 2022). One of the most commercially successful fictional language systems ever created.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Unique approach — rather than replacing specific English curse words, Minionese replaces ALL language, letting audience members infer profanity from tone, context, and physical comedy.
regionalNotes: Used by Minions universally, regardless of location.

exampleQuote.text: BANANA! (said while falling off a building)

realWorldEuphemisms: Nonsense words used as implied profanity

shortDescription: A Na'vi insult meaning 'moron' or 'idiot.' Neytiri calls Jake Sully 'skxawng' when frustrated with his ignorance of Na'vi ways.
etymologyNarrative: From the Na'vi language, a fully constructed language created by linguist Paul Frommer for James Cameron's Avatar. Na'vi has over 3,000 words and complete grammar rules.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used in Avatar (2009) and Avatar: The Way of Water (2022). As Jake learns Na'vi culture, Neytiri uses it less — reflecting his growth.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in the highest-grossing film franchise in history. The Na'vi language gained a dedicated learning community, though 'skxawng' remains its most quoted insult.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: A moderate insult in Na'vi culture. The word is memorable because of Zoe Saldaña's exasperated delivery.
regionalNotes: Used by the Omaticaya clan on Pandora. Other Na'vi clans may have regional variants.

exampleQuote.text: You are like a baby. You are skxawng!

shortDescription: A term used by Ghouls (irradiated, zombie-like humans) for non-irradiated humans. Ranges from affectionate to contemptuous depending on the speaker.
etymologyNarrative: Ghouls are humans mutated by radiation. Their skin is rough and decayed. Calling someone 'smoothskin' highlights the physical difference between them, with an undercurrent of resentment for how Ghouls are treated.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used by Ghoul characters across multiple Fallout games. Tone varies from friendly to deeply hostile depending on the individual Ghoul.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Fallout 3 (2008), Fallout 4 (2015), Fallout 76 (2018), and the Fallout TV series (2024). The TV show brought it to a much wider audience.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Can be either affectionate or hostile. Friendly Ghouls use it casually; feral or resentful Ghouls use it with real venom. Parallels how minority groups may use terms for the majority that range from neutral to bitter.
regionalNotes: Used throughout the Fallout Wasteland wherever Ghouls exist.

exampleQuote.text: Watch yourself around here, smoothskin.

shortDescription: Grunkle Stan's go-to substitute for profanity. Gravity Falls had characters clearly about to swear before substituting with food-based exclamations. Stan once says 'Son of a—' before the scene cuts.
etymologyNarrative: Part of the Disney Channel's content restrictions turned into comedy. The show made a running joke of characters almost swearing and then using absurd food-based substitutes. Creator Alex Hirsch has confirmed the characters would be swearing if they could.
semanticDriftTimeline: Running joke throughout both seasons. The show's approach to implied profanity became a beloved part of its humor.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout Gravity Falls (2012-2016). The show's clever approach to implied profanity influenced subsequent Disney animated shows.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Gravity Falls turned content restrictions into art. The audience always knows what's really being said, and the substitutions themselves became iconic catchphrases.
regionalNotes: Gravity Falls, Oregon.

exampleQuote.text: Hot Belgian waffles! Wait — I'm alone. I can swear for real! SON OF A—

englishEquivalent: I am in great pain, please help me (hidden meaning)
shortDescription: Rick's seemingly nonsensical catchphrase is actually Birdperson's language for 'I am in great pain, please help me.' What seems like a fun exclamation is actually a cry for help.
etymologyNarrative: In-universe, it's from Birdperson's language. Rick learned it and uses it as a catchphrase, hiding his existential despair behind what sounds like a party exclamation. The reveal of its true meaning is one of the show's most emotionally devastating moments.
semanticDriftTimeline: Introduced as a fun catchphrase. Its true meaning is revealed mid-season, completely recontextualizing every previous use. Later, Rick stops using it, suggesting character growth.
usageHistoryNarrative: Introduced in Rick and Morty Season 1 (2014). The catchphrase became massively popular before its true meaning was revealed, adding layers of melancholy to a comedy catchphrase.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Unique trajectory — starts as seemingly meaningless party talk, is revealed to be a genuine cry for help in an alien language. Became a cultural phenomenon and is often cited in discussions of hidden depression.
regionalNotes: Used by Rick across all dimensions in the multiverse.

exampleQuote.text: Wubba lubba dub-dub! (Translation: I am in great pain, please help me.)

relatedWords: Shazbot — alien catchphrase with hidden depth

firstAppearance: The Maze Runner by James Dashner (2009) / Film (2014)
shortDescription: The Gladers' primary expletive, replacing both the F-word and the S-word. Used in 'shuck-face' (insult), 'shucking' (adjective), and as a standalone exclamation.
etymologyNarrative: Created by the Gladers — teenagers with wiped memories trapped in the Maze. Having lost their original language memories, they developed new profanity. 'Shuck' may derive from 'shucking' (removing corn husks — tedious, frustrating work).
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the book trilogy and film series. Part of the Glader slang system that also includes 'klunk' (shit), 'shank' (person), and 'slim it' (calm down).
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Maze Runner trilogy (2009-2016) and the film series (2014-2018). The YA target audience made invented profanity necessary.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Strong within the Glader community. As a YA series, the invented profanity lets characters express genuine fury without alienating younger readers or triggering rating concerns.
regionalNotes: Used exclusively within the Glade and by escaped Gladers.

exampleQuote.text: What the shuck are you doing, you shuck-face?

firstAppearance: The Maze Runner by James Dashner (2009)
shortDescription: Glader slang for excrement, both literal and figurative. The onomatopoeic quality suggests something heavy and unpleasant hitting the ground.
etymologyNarrative: Part of the Glader slang system. The word's sound evokes something heavy and unpleasant — 'klunk' sounds like something dropping into a toilet. Created by memory-wiped teenagers developing their own language.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent through the trilogy.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Maze Runner trilogy.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Moderate scatological term. Less versatile than 'shuck' but fills an important niche in the Glader vocabulary.
regionalNotes: Used within the Glade.

exampleQuote.text: That plan is a bunch of klunk.

firstAppearance: The Tracey Ullman Show shorts (1987) / The Simpsons S1E1 (1989)
shortDescription: Homer's signature exclamation of frustration. While not replacing a specific curse word, it functions as Homer's instinctive profanity — the sound he makes when everything goes wrong.
etymologyNarrative: Dan Castellaneta based it on Jimmy Finlayson's drawn-out 'Dooohh!' from Laurel and Hardy films. The script originally just said 'annoyed grunt.' Castellaneta's delivery made it iconic.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used throughout all 35+ seasons of The Simpsons. Has been used thousands of times. Added to the Oxford English Dictionary in 2001.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used since 1987. Added to the Oxford English Dictionary in 2001. One of the most recognized fictional exclamations in world culture, transcending language barriers.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Unique — it's not a substitute for a specific swear word but functions as Homer's universal expression of frustration. Its addition to the OED legitimized it as a real English word.
regionalNotes: Springfield. Used by Homer in every conceivable situation.

exampleQuote.text: D'oh!

shortDescription: The signature expletive of the Red (lowest caste) population on Mars. A compound of 'bloody' and 'damn' fused into a single intensifier. Marks the speaker as low-born.
etymologyNarrative: Reds are descended from Irish colonists, and 'bloody' is their emphatic prefix — an inheritance from Irish/British English. Combined with 'damn,' it became the Red working class's all-purpose curse. Its use is a class marker: saying 'bloodydamn' instantly identifies you as low-born.
semanticDriftTimeline: Central to Red Rising's class system. When Darrow infiltrates Gold society, he must retrain himself to say 'gorydamn' instead. The linguistic shift symbolizes his identity struggle — every slip back to 'bloodydamn' risks exposing him.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout all six Red Rising novels (2014-present). Became the series' most iconic word. Fans adopted it enthusiastically.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Deeply tied to class identity. 'Bloodydamn' is not just a curse — it's a social marker. Using it in Gold company is like revealing your entire caste history in a single word. The trajectory tracks with Darrow's arc of code-switching between classes.
regionalNotes: Red communities on Mars (the mines of Lykos and other Red settlements). Strictly low-Color vocabulary.

exampleQuote.text: You're a bloodydamn odd lark, Matteo.

shortDescription: The Gold (ruling class) equivalent of 'bloodydamn.' Same function, different word — and the difference marks your entire social identity. Darrow must learn to say this instead of 'bloodydamn' to pass as Gold.
etymologyNarrative: The Golds replaced 'bloody' with 'gory' as their emphatic prefix, perhaps reflecting their warrior culture's comfort with gore. 'Gorydamn' is the refined version of the same curse — same intensity, different class register.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used by Golds throughout the series. The explicit scene where Matteo slaps Darrow every time he says 'bloodydamn' instead of 'gorydamn' is one of the series' most memorable passages.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Red Rising series. The bloodydamn/gorydamn dichotomy is one of the series' most celebrated worldbuilding details.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Functions as a class shibboleth. The word itself isn't more or less taboo than 'bloodydamn' — it's simply the version the powerful use, making it socially acceptable while the identical meaning in Red dialect is stigmatized.
regionalNotes: Gold society across the Solar System. Used in the Institute, the Citadel, and all Gold social settings.

exampleQuote.text: A Gold would rather die than use that slithering mineslang. 'Gorydamn' or 'gory'; and 'slag' instead of 'squab.'

shortDescription: Another class-divided pair: 'slag' is the Gold adjective roughly meaning 'goddamn' while 'squab' is the Red equivalent. Using the wrong one betrays your origins.
etymologyNarrative: Part of Red Rising's systematic class-based vocabulary. The series builds an entire dual-language system where nearly every curse has a Red and Gold version.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the series. Part of the larger system of Red vs. Gold vocabulary that includes bloodydamn/gorydamn, slag/squab, gob/mouth, etc.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Red Rising series (2014-present).
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Reinforces the series' core theme: language is a tool of oppression. The ruling class doesn't just control resources — they control which words are acceptable.
regionalNotes: Slag: Gold society. Squab: Red mining communities.

exampleQuote.text: Every time you say 'squab,' I will kick you in the scrotum.

shortDescription: An insult for Golds who lack a Peerless Scar — meaning they never proved themselves in combat. They drown in luxury and pleasure instead. Among the warrior elite, it's devastating.
etymologyNarrative: In Gold warrior culture, the Peerless Scar (earned through combat) is the mark of true status. Golds without one are 'Pixies' — beautiful, delicate, and useless. The fairy connotation implies they're not real warriors.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the series. Carries increasingly political weight as the revolution progresses.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Red Rising series.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Within Gold warrior culture, being called a Pixie questions your fundamental worth. It's saying you've never been tested and therefore don't matter.
regionalNotes: Gold society. Especially cutting in military and political contexts.

exampleQuote.text: Don't listen to that Pixie. He's never even earned his Scar.

shortDescription: A Rim term for outsiders/foreigners, directly derived from the Japanese 'gaijin.' Used by the isolationist Rim Dominion against Core dwellers.
etymologyNarrative: Explicitly derived from Japanese 'gaijin' (外人, foreigner). The Rim culture in Red Rising has strong Japanese influences, and this linguistic inheritance reflects centuries of cultural evolution on the outer planets.
semanticDriftTimeline: Introduced in Iron Gold as the series expanded to the outer Solar System. Carries the weight of Rim isolationism.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Iron Gold (2018) and subsequent books.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Carries xenophobic weight similar to its Japanese-language ancestor. The Rim considers Core-worlders decadent and corrupt.
regionalNotes: Rim Dominion (outer Solar System). Not used in Core Gold society.

exampleQuote.text: We don't need gahja telling us how to run our worlds.

firstAppearance: The Maze Runner by James Dashner (2009)
shortDescription: Glader slang for 'person.' Can be neutral ('that shank over there') or insulting ('stupid shank') depending on context and tone. One of the Gladers' most-used terms.
etymologyNarrative: Part of the Glader vocabulary that developed among memory-wiped teenagers. May derive from 'shank' (a makeshift weapon) — in the Glade, everyone is potentially dangerous.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the trilogy. Used by all Gladers regardless of status.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Maze Runner trilogy (2009-2016) and films (2014-2018).
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mildly vulgar when used as an insult. Neutral when used as a general term for 'person.' The dual nature mirrors real-world slang like 'dude.'
regionalNotes: Used exclusively within the Glade and by escaped Gladers.

exampleQuote.text: Listen up, you shanks. We need to figure this out.

firstAppearance: The Maze Runner by James Dashner (2009)
shortDescription: Glader command to be quiet or calm down. More forceful than a polite request but less vulgar than 'shut the shuck up.'
etymologyNarrative: Part of the Gladers' invented vocabulary. May derive from 'slim' (to reduce) — slim down your noise, slim your attitude.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the trilogy.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Maze Runner series.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mild imperative. The Glader equivalent of 'cool it' or 'chill.'
regionalNotes: Used within the Glade.

exampleQuote.text: Slim it, Minho. We need to think.

firstAppearance: The Maze Runner by James Dashner (2009)
shortDescription: What the Gladers call any new arrival to the Maze. The term implies ignorance and vulnerability — greenies don't know the rules, the dangers, or how anything works.
etymologyNarrative: From 'green' meaning inexperienced (as in 'greenhorn'). Each month, a new boy arrives in the Glade via the Box with no memories. They're 'green' in every sense — new, raw, and naive.
semanticDriftTimeline: Each new arrival is called Greenie until the next one shows up. Thomas's arc from Greenie to leader drives the first book.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Maze Runner. Thomas being called 'Greenie' establishes his outsider status and creates dramatic tension.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mild but dismissive. Being a Greenie means your opinions don't count yet. It's less an insult and more a status marker, but it's used with condescension.
regionalNotes: Used within the Glade. Each Greenie eventually graduates to a named role.

exampleQuote.text: Day one, Greenie. Take it all in.

shortDescription: A derogatory term used mainly by Dauntless for members of Abnegation, the selfless faction. Implies they're rigid, repressed, and boring.
etymologyNarrative: From 'stiff' as in rigid and uptight. Abnegation members wear plain gray clothes, eat simple food, and deny themselves pleasure. To the thrill-seeking Dauntless, they're impossibly repressed — hence 'Stiff.'
semanticDriftTimeline: Used throughout the Divergent trilogy. Tris (born Abnegation, transferred to Dauntless) is called 'Stiff' as a way to remind her she doesn't belong.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Divergent trilogy (2011-2013) and films (2014-2016). The most recognizable piece of faction-specific slang from the series.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Functions as a caste slur within the faction system. Not the strongest insult but carries real social weight — it tells someone they're in the wrong place and should go back where they came from.
regionalNotes: Used primarily by Dauntless members. Other factions may use it but less frequently.

exampleQuote.text: What's a Stiff like you doing in Dauntless?

shortDescription: A Dauntless insult meaning coward, combining 'pansy' with 'cake' (as in 'piece of cake' — too easy). Uriah tries to revive it as slang, annoying everyone around him.
etymologyNarrative: An older Dauntless insult that fell out of fashion. Uriah's attempt to bring it back is played for comedy — everyone groans when he says it. It's a meta-joke about how invented slang can feel forced.
semanticDriftTimeline: Uriah keeps trying to make 'pansycake' happen throughout Insurgent. It never catches on. This is itself a commentary on how slang works.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Insurgent (2012). Notable as an in-universe example of failed slang revival — even the characters think it sounds stupid.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Intentionally corny. Roth used 'pansycake' to show that not all invented words land. It's more cringe than cutting within the story itself.
regionalNotes: Dauntless compound, but archaic. Uriah is the only one who uses it.

exampleQuote.text: Don't be such a pansycake.

firstAppearance: Ender's Game by Orson Scott Card (1985) / Film (2013)
shortDescription: The human slang term for the Formics (insectoid aliens). While 'bugger' is a real English word (a mild British profanity), in this context it's repurposed as a species slur that dehumanizes the alien enemy.
etymologyNarrative: Combines the aliens' insect-like appearance ('bug') with the existing English profanity 'bugger.' The dual meaning works — it's both descriptive (they look like bugs) and dismissive (they're not worth respecting). The term helps maintain the dehumanization needed to train children to commit genocide.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used casually throughout the book. After Ender discovers the truth about the Formics, the term takes on tragic irony — the 'buggers' were intelligent, empathetic beings.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Ender's Game (1985) and the film (2013). The book's exploration of how dehumanizing language enables violence is one of its central themes.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Central to the book's moral argument. 'Bugger' makes it easy for humanity to treat the Formics as vermin rather than people. When the truth is revealed, the reader realizes they too accepted the dehumanizing term uncritically.
regionalNotes: Used throughout the human military and civilian population of the Ender's Game universe.

exampleQuote.text: The Buggers are coming, and there's nothing we can do about it except fight.

shortDescription: A future-Los Angeles exclamation used in Marie Lu's Legend series. A compressed form of 'goddamn' that has evolved naturally in the Republic's street dialect.
etymologyNarrative: A natural linguistic evolution of 'goddamn' in a future American English dialect. The Republic (a dystopian future Western US) has developed its own slang, and 'goddy' is its most distinctive profanity.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used consistently through the Legend trilogy. Part of the Republic's distinctive dialect.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Legend trilogy (2011-2013). Part of Marie Lu's worldbuilding of a future militaristic American state.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Moderate profanity. Feels authentic as a natural evolution of American English rather than a completely invented word.
regionalNotes: The Republic (former Western United States).

exampleQuote.text: That was a goddy stupid thing to do.

shortDescription: Republic street slang for something bad, worthless, or disgusting. Used as both noun ('that's tross') and adjective ('tross luck').
etymologyNarrative: Part of the Republic's evolved street dialect in Marie Lu's Legend series. No specific real-world etymology — it evolved as future American street slang.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent through the trilogy. Part of Day's street vocabulary that distinguishes him from the elite June.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in the Legend trilogy (2011-2013).
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Moderate street slang. Like 'bloodydamn' vs. 'gorydamn' in Red Rising, the Republic's street slang serves as a class marker.
regionalNotes: Republic streets and underground. Rarely used by upper-class characters.

exampleQuote.text: This whole situation is absolute tross.

englishEquivalent: Watch your mouth / Mind your words
notableSpeaker: Various Community members, especially Jonas's parents
firstAppearance: The Giver by Lois Lowry (1993) / Film (2014)
shortDescription: Not a curse word but its opposite — the Community's mechanism for eliminating strong language and, by extension, strong feeling. When someone uses imprecise or emotional language, they're corrected with this phrase. It represents linguistic control as social control.
etymologyNarrative: In the Community of The Giver, all strong emotion has been suppressed through medication and social conditioning. 'Precision of language' is the enforcement mechanism — children are trained from birth to never use hyperbole, slang, or emotional language. It's profanity-prevention as dystopia.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used throughout the novel. Jonas begins to chafe against it as he receives memories of genuine emotion. His growing desire to use imprecise, emotional language parallels his awakening.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in The Giver (1993) and the 2014 film. One of the novel's most quoted concepts in classroom discussions about dystopian literature.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: The inverse of fictional profanity — a world where all strong language has been eliminated. Including it here because it represents the ultimate logical endpoint of censoring profanity: if you remove the ability to curse, you remove the ability to feel.
regionalNotes: The Community. Universal and strictly enforced.

exampleQuote.text: Jonas, precision of language, please. You do not 'love.' You 'enjoy.'

firstAppearance: The Hunger Games by Suzanne Collins (2008) / Film (2012)
shortDescription: A person whose tongue has been cut out as punishment for rebellion against the Capitol. The word itself comes from Latin 'a' (without) + 'vox' (voice). Being called an Avox reduces a person to their punishment — they are defined entirely by what was taken from them.
etymologyNarrative: From Latin: 'a-' (without) + 'vox' (voice). The Capitol literally removes traitors' voices, then labels them by that absence. The word is both a description and a dehumanization — Avoxes are servants treated as non-persons.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used throughout the trilogy. Katniss recognizes an Avox she saw captured, adding personal horror to the concept. By Mockingjay, the Avoxes become symbols of Capitol cruelty.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout The Hunger Games trilogy (2008-2010) and films (2012-2015). One of the most chilling concepts in the series.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Maximum dehumanization. The word doesn't sound like a slur — it sounds clinical, which makes it worse. The Capitol has bureaucratized cruelty so thoroughly that even their label for silenced people sounds like a filing category.
regionalNotes: Used throughout Panem, especially in the Capitol where Avoxes serve as mute servants to the wealthy.

exampleQuote.text: Don't look at the Avox. Don't speak to them. They're nobody.

franchise: A Court of Thorns and Roses (ACOTAR)
firstAppearance: A Court of Thorns and Roses by Sarah J. Maas (2015)
shortDescription: An oath invoking the Cauldron — the primordial magical artifact that created the fae. 'Cauldron boil me' and 'Cauldron save me' are the most common forms. Since the Cauldron is a real, powerful object in this world, invoking it carries genuine weight.
etymologyNarrative: The Cauldron is the source of all fae magic and creation in the ACOTAR universe. Swearing by it is like swearing by God — except the Cauldron is a demonstrably real, dangerous artifact that can literally unmake people.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used consistently through all five ACOTAR books. The Cauldron becomes central to the plot in later books, making oaths invoking it increasingly literal and terrifying.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the ACOTAR series (2015-present). One of the most popular YA/NA fantasy series currently running, with a massive BookTok following.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Gains weight as the series progresses. In early books, 'Cauldron boil me' feels like a casual oath. By A Court of Wings and Ruin, when the Cauldron literally transforms characters, the phrase becomes chillingly real.
regionalNotes: Used by fae across all courts (Spring, Night, Dawn, etc.) in Prythian.

exampleQuote.text: Cauldron boil me, what have you done?
exampleQuote.source: A Court of Thorns and Roses (ACOTAR)

franchise: A Court of Thorns and Roses (ACOTAR)
firstAppearance: A Court of Thorns and Roses by Sarah J. Maas (2015)
shortDescription: An oath invoking the Mother, a divine figure in the ACOTAR cosmology. Used by both mortal and fae characters as a milder exclamation than Cauldron-based oaths.
etymologyNarrative: The Mother is the divine feminine principle in the ACOTAR cosmology, often paired with the Cauldron. Invoking her is like invoking the Virgin Mary — a gentler oath than taking the Cauldron's name.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the series. A milder oath used alongside the stronger Cauldron oaths.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the ACOTAR series.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mild blasphemy. The gentler of the ACOTAR oath options.
regionalNotes: Used across Prythian and the mortal lands.

exampleQuote.text: Mother above, Rhysand. What were you thinking?
exampleQuote.source: A Court of Thorns and Roses (ACOTAR)

englishEquivalent: N/A — profanity has lost its power
notableSpeaker: Various characters in a post-mortal society
shortDescription: In Scythe's post-mortal world, death-related curses have lost their sting. Nobody fears death because everyone can be revived. The series explores how 'deadish' (temporarily dead, to be revived) replaces 'dead' — and how profanity around death becomes meaningless.
etymologyNarrative: In a world where death has been conquered, words like 'kill' and 'die' lose their taboo. Shusterman coined 'deadish' for the state of temporary death, and explored how a society without mortal fear would need new sources of linguistic shock.
semanticDriftTimeline: A core worldbuilding concept. The evolution from 'dead' (permanent) to 'deadish' (temporary) to 'gleaned' (permanently killed by a Scythe — the only real death) shows how language tracks with cultural fear.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Arc of a Scythe trilogy (2016-2019).
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Brilliant meta-commentary on profanity itself. If death isn't scary, death-words aren't profane. Only 'gleaned' (truly killed by a Scythe) carries weight — because it's the only form of real death left.
regionalNotes: Used worldwide in the post-mortal society.

exampleQuote.text: Relax, you were only deadish for three days.

realWorldEuphemisms: N/A — represents a world where death is no longer scary

notableSpeaker: Percy Jackson, Annabeth Chase, various demigods
firstAppearance: The Lightning Thief by Rick Riordan (2005) / Film (2010) / TV (2023)
shortDescription: Demigods pluralize 'God' to 'gods' because in their world, the Greek gods are literally real. This small linguistic shift signals that the speaker knows the truth about the mythological world.
etymologyNarrative: In a world where Greek gods are real and active, monotheistic expressions don't apply. Demigods naturally pluralize to 'gods.' It's a subtle but consistent worldbuilding detail that Rick Riordan maintains throughout all his mythology series.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout all Percy Jackson books and spin-offs (Heroes of Olympus, Trials of Apollo, etc.). Characters sometimes catch themselves saying 'God' singular and correct to 'gods.'
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Percy Jackson universe (2005-present). The Disney+ TV series (2023-present) maintains the convention. One of the most widely recognized pieces of YA fantasy vocabulary.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Extremely mild. It's not even truly profane — it's a theological correction. But its consistency across the series makes it a defining feature of the demigod vocabulary and a beloved fan identifier.
regionalNotes: Used by all demigods, regardless of which god is their parent. Camp Half-Blood, Camp Jupiter, and the wider mythological world.

exampleQuote.text: Oh my gods, Percy. What did you do now?

firstAppearance: Percy Jackson series by Rick Riordan (2005+)
shortDescription: Demigods substitute 'Hades' for 'Hell' because Hades is literally the god of the Underworld in their reality. It functions identically to 'what the hell' but is theologically accurate.
etymologyNarrative: In the Percy Jackson world, the Greek underworld is real, and Hades rules it. Using his name as a curse is actually riskier than saying 'hell' — you might attract the attention of a real god. Despite this, demigods use it casually.
semanticDriftTimeline: Consistent throughout the series. Other god-name substitutions include 'Styx!' (invoking the river of oaths) and 'Holy Hephaestus!'
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Percy Jackson and Heroes of Olympus series.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Mild by any standard, but the in-universe implication is actually more dangerous than real-world blasphemy — you're casually invoking a deity who can actually hear you and might take offense.
regionalNotes: Used by Greek demigods. Roman demigods at Camp Jupiter have their own variants (invoking Roman god names).

exampleQuote.text: What the Hades is that thing?

englishEquivalent: My lord / term of supreme respect
firstAppearance: Shadow and Bone by Leigh Bardugo (2012) / Netflix series (2021)
shortDescription: A Ravkan term of extreme reverence meaning 'my sovereign.' Used for the Darkling and the most powerful Grisha. Its use signals total submission to authority.
etymologyNarrative: From a Ravkan vocabulary influenced by Russian. Leigh Bardugo built the Grishaverse with Slavic-inspired languages. 'Moi soverenyi' carries the weight of centuries of autocratic culture.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used by Grisha loyal to the Darkling. After his true nature is revealed, the term becomes loaded — those who still use it signal allegiance to darkness.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used throughout the Shadow and Bone trilogy (2012-2014) and the Netflix adaptation (2021-2023). Part of the Grishaverse's rich Ravkan vocabulary.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Not profanity per se, but its misuse or ironic use functions as a form of blasphemy within the Grisha power structure.
regionalNotes: Used in Ravka, the Russia-inspired nation in the Grishaverse.

exampleQuote.text: Moi soverenyi, the volcra are attacking.

franchise: Avatar: The Last Airbender / Legend of Korra
shortDescription: In Aang's time (100 years prior), 'flameo' was a Fire Nation greeting meaning 'hello' or 'great.' By Korra's era, Lin Beifong uses 'What in the flameo happened here?' as a curse. Fan theory suggests Aang's friend Kuzon taught him the word as a prank — it may have always been profane.
etymologyNarrative: Fire Nation slang. 'Flameo hotman' was supposedly a greeting a century before the show's events. Aang learned it from his Fire Nation friend Kuzon before being frozen. By the time he uses it, it's either archaic or was always a dirty joke Kuzon played on him.
semanticDriftTimeline: Remarkable semantic drift: In ATLA (set ~100 AG), Aang uses it as a greeting and everyone looks at him oddly. In Korra (set ~170 AG), Lin Beifong uses it as a clear expletive. This mirrors how real words change meaning over centuries.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in Avatar: The Last Airbender (2005-2008) and The Legend of Korra (2012-2014). 'Flameo hotman' became one of ATLA's most quoted phrases.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: One of the most interesting fictional profanity trajectories: a word that may have always been a curse (disguised as a greeting to trick Aang), or one that genuinely evolved from greeting to profanity over 70 years. Either interpretation enriches the worldbuilding.
regionalNotes: Fire Nation. By Korra's era, it appears to have spread to Republic City's general vocabulary.

exampleQuote.text: Aang: Flameo, hotman! (citizen stares) / Lin Beifong: What in the flameo happened here?
exampleQuote.source: Avatar: The Last Airbender / Legend of Korra

englishEquivalent: Damn! / Crap! / Son of a bitch!
firstAppearance: Avatar: The Last Airbender — The Promise (comic, 2012)
shortDescription: Aang's personal substitute expletive. Combines two random elements (monkeys + feathers) into a nonsensical exclamation — perfectly capturing a 12-year-old Air Nomad monk who was never taught real profanity.
etymologyNarrative: Aang was raised by monks and frozen at age 12. He literally doesn't know real curse words. 'Monkey feathers' is what an innocent kid invents when they need to express frustration. It reveals his sheltered upbringing while being genuinely funny.
semanticDriftTimeline: Introduced in the comics and associated with Aang specifically. Other characters don't adopt it — it's uniquely his.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in the Avatar comics (The Promise, 2012, and sequels). Referenced in fan discussions about Avatar's profanity system.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Deliberately the mildest possible exclamation. That's the point — Aang is a monk-raised child. His profanity reflects his innocence. The contrast between his gentle cursing and Toph's implied vulgar vocabulary is a running joke.
regionalNotes: Air Nomad cultural influence. Only Aang uses it.

exampleQuote.text: Monkey feathers! This is harder than I thought!

firstAppearance: Avatar: The Last Airbender — The Promise (comic, 2012)
shortDescription: A slur used against Fire Nation citizens/firebenders in the post-war era. References the destruction Fire Nation imperialism caused — reducing cities and people to ash.
etymologyNarrative: After the Hundred Year War, resentment toward the Fire Nation runs deep. 'Ash maker' reduces firebenders to the destruction they caused, denying their humanity. It's a post-colonial slur born from generational trauma.
semanticDriftTimeline: Introduced in the post-war comics. Represents the challenge of reconciliation after a century of Fire Nation imperialism.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in The Promise (2012) and subsequent Avatar comics. Explores how prejudice persists even after a war ends.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Deeply charged in-universe. It's the Avatar world's way of exploring how slurs emerge from real historical grievances — the Fire Nation really did burn cities. The word is a product of justified anger hardened into bigotry.
regionalNotes: Used in the former Earth Kingdom territories, especially in the colonies where Fire Nation and Earth Kingdom people lived together.

exampleQuote.text: Go back where you came from, ash maker.

shortDescription: An archaic Fire Nation term of address meaning 'sir' or 'friend.' Aang uses it enthusiastically, not realizing it's 100 years out of date. Citizens react with confusion. By Korra's era, it may have become insulting.
etymologyNarrative: A Fire Nation honorific from a century prior. Like calling someone 'good sir' or 'my good man' today — technically polite but so archaic it sounds bizarre. The Rise of Kyoshi novel confirms 'hotman' and 'hotwoman' were genuine terms 400 years earlier.
semanticDriftTimeline: Archaic in ATLA's timeframe. Aang's use marks him as temporally displaced. By Korra's era, the word is either forgotten or has shifted meaning.
usageHistoryNarrative: Used in ATLA Season 3 (2007). Referenced in The Rise of Kyoshi novel (2019). Part of ATLA's most beloved comedy sequence.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: A masterful worldbuilding detail: showing that language changes over time through a character who was frozen for 100 years. Aang is basically walking around saying 'Greetings, good fellow!' in a modern setting.
regionalNotes: Fire Nation. Archaic by 100 AG. Confirmed as legitimate historical usage by the Kyoshi novels.

exampleQuote.text: Flameo, hotman! Flameo!

englishEquivalent: Asshole (failed attempt at human cursing by an angel)
shortDescription: Castiel's hilariously botched attempt at human profanity while confronting the archangel Michael. An angel who has existed for millennia but has no grasp of human vulgarity, 'assbutt' became one of TV's most beloved invented insults.
etymologyNarrative: Castiel is an angel learning to be human. He understands that insults involve combining body parts with other body parts ('asshole'), but his execution is wrong — he gets 'ass' right but pairs it with 'butt,' creating a redundant nonsense insult. It's the linguistic equivalent of his crooked tie.
semanticDriftTimeline: Used first in S5E22 'Swan Song.' Returned in the 200th episode (S10E5 'Fan Fiction') and S12E7 'Rock Never Dies.' Lucifer himself later admitted he never understood what it meant.
usageHistoryNarrative: First used in 2010. Became one of Supernatural's most iconic moments across its 15-season run. Used on merchandise, in fan art, and in everyday fan vocabulary.
tabooTrajectoryNarrative: Beloved BECAUSE it fails as profanity. Its charm is in the mismatch between the deadly serious situation (confronting Lucifer and Michael) and the laughably inept insult. It humanizes Castiel more than any perfect curse could.
regionalNotes: Used by Castiel. No one else adopts it — Dean repeats it once in confused disbelief.
